channel.title: Best New Albums
channel.description: The most exciting and important new albums being released today.

item.title: My New Band Believe
item.description: Former Black Midi bassist Cameron Picton’s dazzlingly complicated solo debut is a tangle of baroque melodies, chamber-punk arrangements, and garden-path lyrics.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sodomsky

item.title: Wendy Eisenberg
item.description: On a daring new album, the singer-songwriter and guitar virtuoso examines happiness, love, and memory with a forensic curiosity.
item.dc:creator: Jayson Greene

item.title: WOR$T GIRL IN AMERICA
item.description: Smelling like burnt rubber and mall body mist, the pop provocateur’s loud, wild new album swerves hard into club antagonism.
item.dc:creator: Harry Tafoya

item.title: An Undying Love for a Burning World
item.description: On their surprise-released first album in 10 years, the psychedelic Bay Area metal veterans launch a vital and affirming second act with new vocalist Aaron Turner.
item.dc:creator: Grayson Haver Currin

item.title: U
item.description: The former electro-pop enfant terrible swings big on her latest album, compressing all her split personalities and eclectic tastes into a high-gloss, high-stakes gamble to remake pop on her own terms.
item.dc:creator: Walden Green

item.title: Girlfriend
item.description: The reigning champ of indie pop returns with a razor-sharp, refreshingly self-serious album that plays like a travelogue from disarray to recovery.
item.dc:creator: Hattie Lindert

item.title: TIGRAY FUNK
item.description: The Tigray-born, DMV-raised rapper’s latest album is a dazed survey of personal highs and lows, his stream-of-consciousness flow riding roughshod over his producers’ strangest beats.
item.dc:creator: Dash Lewis

item.title: Still Right Here
item.description: The Maryland rapper taps some of SoundCloud’s coolest producers for a full-length solo debut of atmospheric, cybernetic avant-trap. His punch-ins fill more pockets than a tactical vest.
item.dc:creator: Olivier Lafontant

item.title: Blame the Clown
item.description: The New Orleans punk duo channels American country and folk music for a raw, righteous, and impressively original sound. Their second record is Southern garage rock at its best.
item.dc:creator: Nina Corcoran

item.title: As of Now
item.description: The Charlotte rapper’s latest album is the height of his ability. The vignettes of heartbreak and hustling come with absurdist wit and an outstanding selection of beats.
item.dc:creator: Dash Lewis

item.title: Singin’ to an Empty Chair
item.description: Bringing their country-tinted indie rock to boundless new landscapes, the Chicago band returns with their most emotionally affecting and compositionally advanced songs to date.
item.dc:creator: Ivy Nelson

item.title: URGH
item.description: The noise-rock band’s second album is a breakthrough: insidiously catchy, incomprehensibly groovy, and fueled by righteous fury.
item.dc:creator: Walden Green

item.title: Xavier
item.description: The New York rapper’s debut is a cult rap record with big ambition. Cool, chaotic, and hyper-curious, Xav blows up his sound without losing his style.
item.dc:creator: Alphonse Pierre

item.title: Peanut
item.description: The New York musician’s latest album, his first with vocals, is a gently psychedelic tour of his mind, pairing surrealistic lyrics with watery pop-ambient soundscaping.
item.dc:creator: Shaad D’Souza

item.title: Marty Supreme (Original Soundtrack)
item.description: The electronic composer meets the larger-than-life film on its level, building the palette he’s honed over the years into a totalizing prism of sound.
item.dc:creator: Sam Goldner

item.title: I Tried to Tell You
item.description: Embellishing DMV drill with retro R&B, Southern-style storytelling, and striking moments of vulnerability, the DC rapper’s album is simultaneously regional and singular.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Ritchie

item.title: Tranquilizer
item.description: Drawing on a cache of commercial sample CDs, Daniel Lopatin assembles an impossibly dense and transportive electronic album that takes impermanence as its inspiration.
item.dc:creator: Philip Sherburne

item.title: Out the Blue
item.description: On their breakout album, the Baton Rouge rappers wear the championship belt for best duo out of the city, churning out big trash-talking tunes with fat-ass basslines made to set the clubs off.
item.dc:creator: Alphonse Pierre

item.title: LUX
item.description: The Spanish singer’s fourth record is a heartfelt offering of avant-garde classical pop that roars through genre, romance, and religion.
item.dc:creator: Gio Santiago

item.title: Rhythm Immortal
item.description: The Brussels-based producer fled the strictures of techno and drum’n’bass in search of a freer sound. On his astonishing debut LP under a new alias, he seems to rewrite the laws of physics.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ryce

item.title: The BPM
item.description: Brittney Parks’ tense and virtuosic new album documents a life in motion, blending breakups and rebounds, dancefloor euphoria and everyday anxiety.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Felsenthal

item.title: Goodness
item.description: The London singer embraces the darkness on her quiet and ominous full-length debut—it’s one of the most breathtakingly beautiful albums of the year.
item.dc:creator: Philip Sherburne

item.title: Through the Wall
item.description: The Toronto singer returns with a sleek, perfectly executed club record for the late-night crew. Her restrained vocals and velvet hooks are top class.
item.dc:creator: Gio Santiago

item.title: Getting Killed
item.description: The recent metamorphosis of the New York band, led by singer-songwriter Cameron Winter, has produced one of the best, strangest, and most compelling rock records of the year.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sodomsky

item.title: Sysivalo
item.description: On a posthumous album from the pioneering Finnish electronic artist, Mika Vainio’s enduring interest in capturing the vastness of sound is distilled into pieces that feel both atmospheric and tactile.
item.dc:creator: Philip Sherburne

item.title: Blurrr
item.description: The UK singer-songwriter’s sixth album is spectral and breathtaking. It’s a mood record of immense solitude, beauty, and free expression—with a crucial assist from the cellist Oliver Coates.
item.dc:creator: Emma Madden

item.title: Bleeds
item.description: Karly Hartzman leads her North Carolina band in another triumph. The careful songwriting and coiled performances wrestle with the many fiascos of life and love.
item.dc:creator: Walden Green

item.title: Trying Not to Have a Thought
item.description: Philly’s emo-revival heroes are back to show us how it’s done. The band’s first new album in 14 years threads their finger-tapped riffs with familiar camaraderie and new social consciousness.
item.dc:creator: Nina Corcoran

item.title: A Danger to Ourselves
item.description: The Colombian musician trades her typically fantastical subject matter for an album steeped in the immediacy of desire. The theme may be universal, but her sounds are as thrillingly out there as ever.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Ismael Ruiz

item.title: Burnover
item.description: The Vermont singer’s pretty great second album has got wit, romance, and sublime songwriting. It’s an unassuming collection of tunes that belongs in the heartland slacker rock canon.
item.dc:creator: Grace Robins-Somerville
